docs.title: Eirath Adventures
docs.text:
A thousand years ago the Celestial Armies and the Demonic Legions came to Eirath to fight over it. Neither of them asked the world's permission. So the world fought back — indiscriminately, against both — and when the War of Dominion finally guttered out, gods lay dead on either side and the blood of angels, demons, and primordials had soaked so deeply into the soil that it began, in time, to grow people. Every mortal race of Eirath is what that war left behind.

The war is over. The wounds are not. Battlefields still split open into Splinters — raw planar wounds that vent vengeful celestials and raving demons into the countryside. The center of the world is unmapped, and the scholars who fund expeditions into it rarely see their expeditions come home. Most people never go looking. They farm, they fish, they cut timber, and they stay as far from the old battle-sites as a living can be made.

The party is not most people. This is their record — who they are, what they've done, and the world that keeps testing them.

docs.title: The Graylight Veil
docs.text:
The trees of the Graylight Forest grew back after the war. Something else stayed. A demonic taint is woven into the air itself, and the result is a permanent low haze that bends light and memory. Travelers cough, lose their footing on paths they've walked for years, hear whispering in the branches, and cannot shake the certainty that they are being followed through empty clearings.

The locals do not call it a curse. A curse can be lifted. They call it the Graylight Veil, and they live with it, the way you live with a scar.

docs.title: Timberfall
docs.text:
On the forest's western edge stands Timberfall, a frontier logging town founded by five noble families who all wanted the same thing: the right to cut the Graylight down and sell it. It has not gone the way they planned. Felled trees regrow overnight. Wood creaks at night in something too close to voices. Supply routes wander off the map. And still the families send more laborers in — among them the Silverbriers, who are cheap, careless, and famous for sending untrained people deeper into the woods than anyone should go.

What Timberfall has to offer is thin: a bad ale at The Fat Goat, empty shelves at Ol' Marnie's, and the small, poor Church of the Dawn, whose healer gives away his potions rather than sell them. Some say the town is not harvesting the forest. They say the forest is testing the town.

docs.title: The Wider World
docs.text:
North of the Graylight, past the Frost Horn Mountains, the fortress-nation of Gil'Serith judges every outsider who approaches and admits almost none — mithral-rich GoldHearth carved into the mountain, Frost Ring City filtering the world at its gate, and Fort Stillwater, a prison that pays down its inmates' sentences in expeditions nobody expects them to survive.

South, the Cinderspire Mountains are not mountains at all but the fossilized spine of a slain demon leviathan, still faintly warm, still faintly breathing. Its maw tore the Hellmouth into the world and the world has been bleeding there ever since. Around it: the tar-clan city of Blackfoot, the treacherous Tiefling councils of Emberhold, the scavenger-metropolis of Allsbhaad stacked out of celestial wreckage, and far east, Bastion, the last town before the Valley of the Dead Gods.

All of it is reachable. Almost none of it is safe.

docs.title: The Party
docs.text:
Grano, Icarus, Vorhan Aru Nyshara, and Wilonet — four people who found each other in Timberfall with nothing much between them but a job nobody sane would take.

The wiki has the people and the places. The sessions below have what happened to them.

docs.title: Sessions
docs.text: # Session Date 1 Twelve Missing 2026-07-05

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Three strangers — a scarred human mercenary, a drow who has outlived most of the town, and a dwarf who is the last living thing to walk out of his monastery — reach for the same job posting outside the Silverbrier Estate at the same moment. Twelve loggers, gone two weeks into the Graylight Forest. Twenty gold a head, and only if they get there before the other rescue party does. They spend a day shaking what little truth Timberfall has out of it, walk into the fog at first light, and discover on the first evening that the wolves in the Graylight do not behave like wolves.

docs.title: Key Events
docs.text:
• The posting. Twelve loggers — orcs, humans, a gnome or two — sent two or three days deep by the Silverbrier family and unheard from since. In a town of fifty or sixty people, that is a fifth of the population, and nothing like it has ever happened before.
  • Icarus was supposed to be there. He had been hired as the logging camp's security escort and arrived two weeks late from Deep Hollow. The camp went out without him. The head of security — a smug, salt-and-pepper man who stands at the gate and never goes past it — seemed to consider this Icarus's problem rather than the family's.
  • A bad deal, badly negotiated. Twenty gold per surviving logger, split three ways. Icarus tried to talk it up to thirty and failed. The head of security's counteroffer was that he could go out alone and keep the others' shares if they died — and that if they killed each other out there, nobody at the estate would care.
  • They are in a race and nobody told them. A previous rescue party of four went out roughly a week ago. Whichever group brings back living loggers first gets paid. The other gets nothing.
  • The map. A hand-drawn thing with random trees scattered across it and a single X, which the head of security cheerfully admitted he had only recently learned to draw.
  • The Fat Goat. At the tavern they found Jonas Dawn, the town healer, alone at a table with an untouched ale and a leg jittering hard enough to hear across the room. His sister Miriam is in the first rescue party. She negotiated thirty-five gold a survivor and intends to hand every coin of it to their meager temple. She has been gone a week.
  • Icarus assaulted the healer. Frustrated at getting nothing useful, he grabbed Jonas by the shirt and threatened to kill him. Wilonet slid in behind with a dagger drawn, then talked him down and smoothed it over. Jonas gave up healing potions from the church anyway — one to Icarus, one pocketed, and a third to Wilonet with the instruction: don't use it on him.
  • "The dawn gives me power." Jonas said it to the man who had just threatened his life, and did not seem to be speaking figuratively. Grano identified the Church of the Dawn from the pendant and the potions — redemption, rebirth, beginning again.
  • Something is riding Icarus. Jonas's parting blessing, "may the light find you," was answered from inside Icarus's own skull by a voice that was not his: "Good. We don't want the light."
  • Ol' Marnie's. Old Marnie, an elf ancient even by elven standards and an old friend of Wilonet's, gave them the only real intelligence they got all day: the forest plays tricks on the mind, the forest does not want to be cut down, the spirits in it have lain dormant a long time, and the deeper you push the angrier they get. His hard advice: don't trust everything you see out there. He had no potions left to sell — he'd given his stock to Jonas. He asked them to bring back nightroot if they saw it, and sketched the dark blue vine for them.
  • Icarus went shopping without paying. While Wilonet talked, he cased the shelves and marked a bottle that looked empty until the light caught it — a clear liquid that moved on its own. He could not identify it. He has announced, out loud, that he intends to come back and steal it.
  • Into the Graylight. They camped at the treeline and went in at first light. Grano found the loggers' route: an easy path, and cart tracks in the mud. Wilonet spotted one of her own old hunter's traps a step ahead of Icarus's boot, stopped him, and then set it off with a thrown rock to show him what he'd nearly walked into. He thanked her. She hated him slightly less. He did not reciprocate.
  • The wolves. Howling at dusk, fog thickening. Everyone agreed they were ordinary wolves. They were not. These blink — vanishing mid-lunge and reappearing behind you, thirty feet away, out of nothing. Nobody in Timberfall has ever seen a wolf do that.
  • The fight. One savaged Icarus for seven damage — he had eleven hit points, an armor class of eleven, and had forgotten he owned a defensive spell. At four hit points he disengaged and hid behind a tree. Grano took jaws on his chainmail, burned the beast with sacred flame, and cracked it with his mace. Wilonet killed two: one beheaded so cleanly the body blinked out of existence before it dropped, and one opened from throat to belly while she held eye contact with the last wolf standing. It tried to teleport away. It couldn't.

docs.title: Memorable Moments
docs.text:
• Forty gold. Wilonet, rolling a natural 20 on perception from across the tavern, heard Icarus mocking his own party to Jonas — and answered by announcing to the entire room that the price to rescue Jonas's sister had just gone up to forty gold.
  • Eldritch blast, described. Asked what his magic looked like in that oppressive fog, Icarus said he reaches down, scoops a handful of the gray out of the air, and throws it. It does not glow. It vanishes into the murk.
  • Grano's sacred flame. "Bright, vibrant, cleansing, and plain." It left the wolf's gray fur charred.
  • The rock and the trap. Wilonet openly admitted she considered letting Icarus step into the snare. She saved him instead — and then triggered it in front of his face, just to make the point.
  • "At this point, it's personal." She disemboweled the second wolf from the neck down while staring at the third one. The DM made her roll intimidation. She did not need the advantage.
  • The DM's public service announcement. Delivered mid-shakedown, to a table of first-timers: D&D is not a video game. Murder someone in cold blood and the character you spent ninety minutes building is dead forever.

docs.title: Open Threads
docs.text:
• Twelve loggers, two or three days deep, missing two weeks. Alive is worth twenty gold each. Dead is worth nothing.
  • Miriam Dawn and her three companions — an orc hedge knight, a smug high elf who claims family, and a flashy gnome with black or blue hair — a week gone, and the party's direct competition for the fee.
  • What is in Icarus? Something in him recoiled from a blessing and spoke in the first person plural.
  • The blinking wolves. Nothing in local memory does this. Nobody knows what changed, or how deep it goes.
  • Nightroot, and whatever Old Marnie means to make of it.
  • The bottle on Marnie's shelf — clear, alive, unidentified, and already earmarked for theft.
  • The forest, Marnie says, does not want to be cut down. The party is now three days from anyone who could help them.

docs.title: The Scene
docs.text:
The howling had been ordinary. That was the thing they would come back to afterward, each of them privately: they had all three stopped, and listened, and agreed. Wolves. The Graylight has wolves the way any forest has wolves. You keep a fire and you keep a watch and in the morning you keep walking. Then the first one came out of the ferns at Icarus, and he saw it leave the ground — jaws wide, the wet black of the mouth, all that weight arriving — and he threw his arm up to brace against it, and nothing came. The air closed over the place where the wolf had been, smoothly, like water over a dropped stone. He stood there with his arm raised against an empty forest. He had half a second to be confused, and he spent it.

Then he heard it breathing behind his ear. It took him on the arm — the right one, the one he scoops the gray out of the air with, the one the magic comes out of — and it bit down and worried it, and the sound Icarus made was not a word. Seven points of the eleven he had. He went down into the wet leaves with his blood running out of him in a rope, and he did not get up; he dragged himself backward until there was a tree between him and the thing, and he stayed there. Behind him Grano's mace kindled with a clean white light that did not belong in this place, and the wolf that had opened the man's arm turned, unhurried, to see what else was on offer.

Wilonet was already walking. Not running — walking, short sword low at her side, the way you approach something you have already finished deciding about. Three hundred years in forests, and half of them in this one, and she had never once been afraid of a wolf. The animal blinked toward her and she was not where it arrived. Her blade came up under its jaw and through, one long clean stroke, and the head went one way and the body went the other and then the body simply stopped being there — blinked out mid-fall, gone, and reappeared thirty feet off in the fog to drop into the ferns like a sack of meat. She did not watch it land. She had already turned to the last one, and she opened it from the throat down while she held its eyes, and she made certain it understood. It gathered itself to blink away. Nothing happened. Behind her, from behind his tree, Icarus decided he had been right about her all along and revised nothing else.

docs.title: The Church of the Dawn
docs.text: A faith of redemption, rebirth, and beginning again

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A church whose whole theology is the sunrise: redemption, rebirth, starting anew. Whatever you were before does not have to be what you are now. In a world built out of the corpses of a war between demons and celestials — where every race alive carries some measure of that old blood in it — a faith about starting over is not a small idea.

Grano, examining the potions they were handed, recognized it immediately and knew it well.

docs.title: Sigil
docs.text: A half-risen sun on a white field, small rays coming off it. Jonas Dawn wears it as a pendant over his robes.

docs.title: Presence in Timberfall
docs.text:
Small and poor. The Dawn family has been in Timberfall a long time and runs a meager little temple there. It is not a wealthy institution — Miriam Dawn took the logger-rescue contract intending to hand the entire fee over to fund it, and Jonas gives away the healing potions he brews with Old Marnie rather than sell them.

Their blessing, offered in parting: "May the light find you."

docs.title: Known Members
docs.text:
• Jonas Dawn — healer, brewer of potions, and the one who says the dawn gives him power. It may not be a figure of speech.
  • Miriam Dawn — his sister. Missing in the Graylight Forest.

docs.title: Relationship with the Party
docs.text:
The church has given the party healing potions and asked nothing for them. It is, so far, the only institution in Timberfall that has treated them decently — which did not stop Icarus from grabbing its local priest by the shirt and threatening to kill him.

And there is something else. When Jonas offered Icarus the church's blessing — "may the light find you" — Icarus said he didn't care about the light. And then, unbidden, in his own skull, he heard a thought that was not his:

Good. We don't want the light.

Whatever Icarus's patron is, it has opinions about this church.

docs.title: The Silverbrier Family
docs.text: One of the five founding families of Timberfall

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Timberfall was founded by five wealthy families, all competing for the same thing: the right to cut down the Graylight Forest, haul out the lumber, and sell it. The Silverbriers are one of them, and they have a particular reputation — cheap, careless, and the likeliest of the five to send untrained people further into the woods than anyone should go. As Icarus put it, having worked for them before: they always send you on the dangerous jobs, and they never pay well.

They are the reason twelve people are missing.

docs.title: Leadership
docs.text:
Lady Silverbrier holds the house in Timberfall. She is a second daughter — no inheritance is coming to her — and she was posted out here largely against her will to run the family's logging concern while the rest of them enjoy Deep Hollow. Greedy, and openly indifferent to the common folk. Asked why she'd sent twelve loggers into a forest everyone agrees is deadly without waiting two days for the escort she had already hired, the family's own head of security shrugged: paying idle workers for two weeks would be insane. She has the money. She simply didn't feel like spending it.

The party has not met her. That meeting has been floated, and has not yet happened.

docs.title: Known Members
docs.text: • The head of security — the smug human at the gate who does the family's hiring and none of its risking.

docs.title: Current Activities
docs.text:
• Logging the Graylight, badly and at other people's expense.
  • Recovering the twelve missing loggers — cheaply. They have hired two parties for this now and intend to pay only whichever one produces living loggers first. The party (twenty gold a head) is competing directly against Miriam Dawn's group (thirty-five gold a head), who went out a week ago and have not come back.
  • They will pay nothing for the rescue of the first rescue party.
  • If both parties fail, the plan is simply to hire more desperate labor out of Deep Hollow and keep sending them in until someone figures out what is out there.

docs.title: Relationship with the Party
docs.text:
Employer, grudgingly, on both sides. Icarus has a history with them and it is not a warm one: he was hired as the escort for this expedition, arrived two weeks late, and was fired for it. Wilonet took the job purely for the money and has said so out loud. Grano thinks the whole enterprise is foolishness and has said that out loud, in Dwarvish, to a drow, in front of them.

Icarus is currently quartered in the family's barracks at the estate.

docs.title: The Graylight Forest
docs.text: An ancient forest of dark, dark brown wood and oppressive leaves, drowned at every hour in a pale standing fog that never burns off. The haze is not weather. It is residue — what settled here after the demons of the Abyss and the celestials of the heavens finished using this world as their battlefield, thousands of years ago, and it has never lifted since. The deeper in you go, the fewer feet have gone before you, and the angrier the forest gets.

docs.title: What It Does to You
docs.text: Every account agrees on one thing: the Graylight plays tricks with your head. It shows you things that are not there and hides things that are. The fog makes it hard to see what is directly in front of you. Old Marnie's only real advice, delivered as the party left his hut, was this: don't trust everything you see out there.

docs.title: The Forest Fights Back
docs.text:
The oldest rumor in Timberfall — the one Old Marnie gives the most weight — is that the forest simply does not want to be cut down. The spirits in it have lain dormant a long time. Sometimes, he says, it bites back. And the further in you push, into the parts where almost nobody has ever walked, the more furious those spirits become.

Twelve loggers went two or three days deep, and vanished.

docs.title: Known Dangers
docs.text:
• The wolves. Ordinary wolves range the shallow reaches, and no one thinks much of them. What the party met on the first evening in was not ordinary. They blink — vanishing mid-lunge and reappearing behind you, or thirty feet away. Even beheaded, the body flickers out of existence before it drops. Nothing in Timberfall's memory has ever seen a wolf do this.
  • The fog itself, and whatever it lets you believe.

docs.title: Nightroot
docs.text: A dark blue, vine-like plant that grows somewhere out in the Graylight. Old Marnie forages for it and wants more — he'll turn it into "something" for anyone who brings it in, and sketched it out for the party before they left. What it becomes, he did not say.

docs.title: Ol' Marnie's
docs.text: A cramped alchemical hut on the edge of Timberfall, barely more than a shack, with a hand-lettered sign reading OL' MARNIE'S and shelves of glass crowding every wall. It is the only place in town to buy a potion, and lately there is very little on the shelves worth buying — Old Marnie gave most of his remaining stock to Jonas Dawn days ago, and says he has nothing left to spare.

docs.title: What's On the Shelves
docs.text:
Mostly empty space and dust. But two bottles caught Icarus's eye while he loitered by the shelves, pretending not to case the room:

  • Potions matching the ones Jonas had been handing out — ordinary healing.
  • An odd, near-empty bottle. It looks like it holds nothing at all, until you squint: there is a little clear liquid in the bottom, and it moves when the light from outside catches it. Icarus studied it and came away with no idea what it was. It has not been identified, and it has not been stolen.

Both Wilonet and the shopkeeper made the consequences of theft in a small town extremely clear. Icarus's position, stated aloud, is that he is probably going to try anyway.

docs.title: What Happened Here
docs.text: The party came to Old Marnie for information about the Graylight Forest, on Jonas's recommendation. They got the local wisdom — the fog lies to you, the forest doesn't want to be cut, the spirits get angrier the deeper you go — and a standing request: bring back any nightroot he can turn into something useful. He drew them a picture of it before they left.

docs.title: The Silverbrier Estate
docs.text: A gated manor house that is, by a comfortable margin, the finest building in Timberfall — which in Timberfall is not saying a great deal, but the gate is real and the guard standing at it is real. Everyone in town knows where it is. The Silverbrier family runs its logging concern out of it, and the job board where the party first laid eyes on one another stands within sight of the gate.

docs.title: The Posting
docs.text:
MISSING — 12 LOGGERS from the Silverbrier family. Pays well. Interested persons should inquire.

It does not pay well. The head of security who stands at the gate settled the party at twenty gold a head for every logger brought back alive, and would not be moved off it — pointedly noting that Icarus, who was supposed to have been escorting those loggers in the first place and turned up two weeks late, had no leverage whatsoever.

He was also perfectly happy to observe that if the party's members killed each other out there, the survivors could simply keep the dead one's cut.

docs.title: Who Holds It
docs.text:
Lady Silverbrier — a second daughter, and therefore a woman with no inheritance coming to her. She was sent out to Timberfall to run the family's logging interest, largely against her will; she would much rather be in Deep Hollow. She is described, by people who have to live near her, as greedy and entirely indifferent to the common folk. It was her call to send twelve loggers into the Graylight with no escort rather than wait two more days and pay them for the delay.

The party has not yet met her. She has not come out to the gate.

docs.title: The Fat Goat
docs.text: Timberfall's one and only tavern — a ramshackle, leaning thing hung with a painted sign of an enormous white goat split cleanly in half. It smells overwhelmingly of mutton, which is also, unsurprisingly, what they sell. Gnomes help each other up onto barstools that were not built for them. A half-elf works behind the bar, endlessly polishing the same glass and staring at nothing in particular.

docs.title: Who Drinks Here
docs.text: Everyone, because there is nowhere else. On the night the party came through, the notable patron was Jonas Dawn — the town healer, sitting alone at a table with an ale at his elbow, one leg jittering under him hard enough that a sharp ear could hear it across the room. He was not drinking because he wanted to.

docs.title: What Happened Here
docs.text:
The party met for the first time at the job board and came here to ask questions before heading into the Graylight. They got their first real thread: Jonas's sister Miriam had gone into the forest with three others roughly a week ago, and had not come back.

They also got their first taste of each other. Icarus grabbed the town healer by the shirt and threatened to kill him. Wilonet — who had heard every word of Jonas's muttering from clear across the room — announced to the whole tavern that the price for rescuing his sister had just gone up to forty gold. It went about as well as that sounds, and Jonas still handed over healing potions on his way out, with instructions about who not to waste one on.

docs.title: Timberfall
docs.text: A logging settlement of barely fifty or sixty souls, hammered together on the eastern edge of the Graylight Forest by five wealthy families who have never once swung an axe themselves. They pay the poor, the desperate, and the merely aimless to walk into the trees and cut, and the trees have begun to take them. Mud streets, a single sagging tavern, a temple barely worth the name, and beyond the last fence the fog stands waiting like a wall.

docs.title: The Five Families
docs.text: Timberfall was founded by five families, all of them competing for the same prize: the right to cut the Graylight down, haul the lumber out, and sell it. The rivalry is the town's real government. The Silverbriers are the family the party has dealings with, and by all accounts they are the worst of the five — the ones most willing to send untrained people too deep for too little coin.

docs.title: Who You'll Find Here
docs.text:
• Jonas Dawn — the town's healer, of the small Church of the Dawn temple. Currently drinking himself sideways at the Fat Goat over his missing sister.
  • Old Marnie — an ancient elf alchemist who keeps a hut at the edge of town and knows more about the forest than anyone living.
  • The Silverbrier head of security — a smug, salt-and-pepper human who stands at the estate gate and does not, under any circumstances, go into the woods himself.

docs.title: Notable Places
docs.text:
• The Fat Goat — the one tavern.
  • Ol' Marnie's — the alchemist's hut.
  • The Silverbrier Estate — the nicest house in a town with no nice houses.

docs.title: The Missing
docs.text:
Twelve loggers — orcs, humans, a gnome or two — were sent two or three days deep into the Graylight and have not been heard from in two weeks. In a town this size that is a fifth of the population. People vanish in the Graylight now and then, one or two at a time, when they wander too far. Twelve at once has never happened before.

A first rescue party of four went out roughly a week ago and has also not returned.

docs.title: Elsewhere
docs.text:
Timberfall is a backwater, and everyone here knows it. The place people would rather be is Deep Hollow, a larger settlement some distance off — where the Silverbriers wish they were stationed, where fresh desperate labor can always be hired, and where a mercenary might be posted between jobs.

South or above, depending on who you ask, stand the Cinderspire Peaks, a hard range of mountains. There was a monastery up there once. There isn't anymore.

docs.title: Jonas Dawn
docs.text: Healer of Timberfall — Church of the Dawn

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The closest thing Timberfall has to a doctor, and by his own cheerful admission not a brave man. Jonas comes from a large family that has been in town a long time and runs the meager little temple here. He spends his days patching up loggers who cut their own thumbs off doing basic work at the treeline, and he is very clear that he considers most of them idiots. He is also, right now, falling apart.

docs.title: Appearance
docs.text: White robes — the vestments of his church — badly stained with alcohol and worn far too long without washing. Around his neck a pendant: a white field bearing a half-risen sun with small rays coming off it. Dark-skinned. When the party found him at the Fat Goat he had an ale at his side and one leg shaking so hard under the table that a drow across the room could hear it.

docs.title: Personality
docs.text:
Nervous, profane, self-aware, and genuinely kind underneath it. He describes himself, without much shame, as "too much of a pansy" to go into the Graylight after his own sister. He calls himself a do-gooder and then immediately clarifies that unlike Miriam he is not an idiot about it. His grief comes out sideways, as complaining.

He does not have much, and he gave away what he had anyway.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text: Get Miriam back. Everything else is noise.

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Miriam Dawn — his sister. She went into the Graylight about a week ago with three others to rescue the missing loggers. He begged her not to go. She went. He is coming apart over it.
  • Icarus — an old friend, or was. Greeted him warmly by name across the tavern. Within a few minutes Icarus had him by the shirt, promising to kill him if he didn't talk. Jonas's parting shot — "the dawn gives me power" — was not entirely a joke, and he made a point of handing the party's healing potions to other people, with instructions not to waste one on the human.
  • Old Marnie — supplies him with potions and helps him brew them. Jonas sent the party on to Marnie for real information about the forest.
  • Wilonet — bought his goodwill back after Icarus burned it, and promised to try to bring his sister home.

docs.title: Session History
docs.text: Twelve Missing — Found shaking alone at the Fat Goat. Told the party about the first rescue party and his sister's part in it, and that Miriam had negotiated thirty-five gold a survivor where the party had settled for twenty. Knew nothing useful about the forest itself and said so — "they said that place is scary, so I just never fucking went in there." Was assaulted by Icarus, defended by the other two, and gave up healing potions from the church regardless. Pointed the party at Old Marnie. Sent Icarus off with "may the light find you," which Icarus did not care for at all — and which something inside Icarus liked even less.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• Is he holding something back? Two separate insight reads came up the same: genuinely terrified, genuinely worried about his sister. Whether that's the whole truth is another matter.
  • He pocketed a second potion rather than hand it over. He is not quite as empty-handed as he claims.

docs.title: Miriam Dawn
docs.text: Missing — Church of the Dawn

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Jonas's sister, and by every account a far better person than anyone else in Timberfall. When the notice went up about the twelve missing loggers, Miriam did not haggle and did not hesitate — she gathered three others and went into the Graylight Forest to bring them home. That was about a week ago. Nobody has heard from her since.

The party has not met her. They have only met the hole she left.

docs.title: Personality
docs.text:
A do-gooder in the sincerest and most inconvenient sense. Her brother begged her to stay — there was more than enough work in town, he told her, patching up people who cut their own thumbs off — and she told him she had to go help those people, because she was better equipped to help than anyone else who might try. He could not argue her out of it.

She was not even going to keep the money. The whole fee was meant to fund the family's threadbare little temple.

docs.title: Her Company
docs.text:
She went out with three others, none of them named to the party so far:

  • An orc, an upstart hedge knight.
  • A high elf woman — not drow — who carried herself with the particular smugness elves are famous for. Said to come from one of Timberfall's founding families.
  • A gnome, very flashy, very outspoken, with black or blue hair.

They negotiated thirty-five gold per surviving logger from the Silverbriers — considerably better than the twenty the party settled for. The Silverbrier head of security has made it clear that if this first party comes back with living loggers, they get paid and the party does not. He has also made it clear that the Silverbriers will pay nothing at all for rescuing them.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Missing, one week. Alive or dead is unknown.

docs.title: Session History
docs.text: Twelve Missing — Learned of, but not encountered. Wilonet, hearing Jonas's grief from across the Fat Goat, announced across the room that the price to bring his sister back had just gone up to forty gold — and then, later and more quietly, promised him she would try to rescue her anyway, as an apology for Icarus.

docs.title: Old Marnie
docs.text: Alchemist — Ol' Marnie's, Timberfall

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: An elf, and old even by the standards of elves — which in a town where a three-hundred-year-old drow counts as the ancient one is saying something. He keeps a cramped alchemical hut at the edge of Timberfall, brews the town's potions, and forages out in the fringes of the Graylight Forest for the things he needs. He has been here a very long time and he knows more about those woods than anyone else still breathing in this town.

docs.title: Personality
docs.text: Dry, unhurried, faintly amused by everyone. He greeted Wilonet as an old friend and Grano politely, and dismissed Icarus with a shrug and the observation that "he's young — most humans are." He is not frightened of the Graylight the way Jonas is. He simply respects it, which is worse.

docs.title: What He Knows
docs.text:
Old Marnie is the party's best source on the forest, and what he gave them was this:

  • The Graylight fucks with your head. It makes you see things that were never there.
  • The oldest rumor, and the one he clearly credits: the forest does not want to be cut down. The spirits in it have been dormant a long time. Sometimes it bites back.
  • The deeper you go, the fewer people have been there — and the angrier the spirits get.
  • His parting advice, and the only thing he'd call hard: don't trust everything you see out there.

docs.title: Nightroot
docs.text: He asked the party to bring back any nightroot they come across — a dark blue, vine-like plant that grows out in the Graylight. He can "turn it into something." He drew them a sketch of it before they left. He did not say what it becomes, and nobody thought to ask.

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Wilonet — a genuine old friend. She has known him for years and vouched for him to the others; she asked to do the talking, and asked the others, pointedly, not to rough him up.
  • Jonas Dawn — he supplies Jonas's potions and helps him brew them. He gave Jonas most of his remaining stock days before the party arrived, which is why he had none to spare when they asked.
  • Miriam Dawn — she came to him asking about the forest the day before she left. He knows exactly how that story is likely to end.
  • Icarus — spent the conversation eyeing the shelves and sizing up what he could steal, and has stated aloud that he intends to try. Marnie has not noticed. Yet.

docs.title: Session History
docs.text: Twelve Missing — Gave the party the only real intelligence they have about the Graylight, and a standing job. Had no potions left to sell.

docs.title: The Silverbrier Head of Security
docs.text:
Gate guard and contract-man — Silverbrier Estate

His name has not yet come up. The party has never asked, and he has never offered.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The human who stands at the gate of the Silverbrier Estate and hands out the family's dirty work. He is the one who posted the notice about the twelve missing loggers, the one who set the price, and the one who will not be going into the Graylight himself under any circumstances — a point he made repeatedly and with obvious satisfaction.

docs.title: Appearance
docs.text: Middle-aged, salt-and-pepper hair, a goatee. Smug. He leans in when he talks, as though letting you in on something.

docs.title: Personality
docs.text:
Cheerfully contemptuous of just about everyone, and unbothered about hiding it. He calls the people he's hiring "brave people — idiots" in the same breath. He barely reads; the map he handed the party is, in his own words, drawn by a man who learned the letter X two weeks ago, and it looks like it. He told Icarus, without lowering his voice, that if the drow and the dwarf died out there the survivors could just keep their shares, and that he did not care in the slightest if they killed each other.

He also does not know what a lawsuit is. Possibly the lady of the house does.

docs.title: What He Wants
docs.text: The loggers back at work — or failing that, back at all. Failing that, more loggers hired cheap out of Deep Hollow, where there are always more desperate people. He is not especially invested in which.

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Lady Silverbrier — his employer. He defers everything of consequence to her and clearly does not want to be the one making decisions.
  • Icarus — has worked for the family before and was supposed to be the security escort for this very logging expedition. He turned up two weeks late. The head of security has not let this go for a single minute of their acquaintance, and used it to shut down Icarus's attempt to negotiate the rate up from twenty gold.

docs.title: Session History
docs.text: Twelve Missing — Hired the party at twenty gold per surviving logger, refused to budge, handed over a nearly useless map, and mentioned — almost in passing — that a previous rescue party of four had already gone out, and that if they came back with survivors first, the party would get nothing.

docs.title: Grano
docs.text:
Player: Darryl · Ancestry: Dwarf · Class: Cleric

Last of his Monastery

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Ninety-five years old, gray-haired, and very quiet. Chainmail, a mace, and a shield, carried with what can only be described as a soft-spoken elegance — he is not a loud man and does not become one under pressure. When the party first gathered at the job board it was Grano who actually asked the useful question: how long have these twelve been missing?

He mostly grumbles. The party has already worked out that when he does say something, it is worth hearing.

docs.title: What He Carries
docs.text:
Grano spent the last seven years alone. He is the last survivor of a monastery somewhere up in the Cinderspire Peaks, where everyone else was killed. He mentioned this once, flatly, to a stranger who had irritated him, and then said nothing further about it.

He has not explained what happened there. Nobody has asked.

docs.title: Faith
docs.text:
His holy magic is bright, vibrant, cleansing, and plain — his own words. His sacred flame falls on a thing and leaves it charred. In a forest full of gray fog and lying half-light, he is the only light in the party that is actually light.

He knew the Church of the Dawn on sight, and recognized its sigil and its theology of redemption and rebirth immediately — a very good roll on a subject he clearly already knew well. Which god he himself serves has not yet been said aloud.

docs.title: Temperament
docs.text: Disinclined to suffer fools, and unbothered about hiding it. Told the Silverbrier head of security, to his face and without heat, "I don't like you at all." Thinks the whole logging enterprise is greed dressed up as commerce — steel is expensive, the war was foolishness, and these people rush off to danger without a thought for anyone involved.

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Wilonet — mutual respect, arrived at almost instantly. They conferred in Dwarvish about whether the job was worth taking before either of them had said a friendly word to the human.
  • Icarus — has said essentially nothing to him. When Jonas Dawn asked Grano to vouch for Icarus, Grano answered honestly: "I don't know him that well."
  • Jonas Dawn — introduced himself politely, asked the real question about why Miriam had gone, and was the one Jonas trusted with a healing potion — along with the instruction not to waste it on Icarus.

docs.title: Session History
docs.text: Twelve Missing — Asked the questions that mattered and let others do the shouting. Identified the Church of the Dawn from its potions and pendant. Scouted the party's route into the Graylight, spotting the cart tracks that marked the loggers' path. Took a wolf's jaws on his armor without much complaint, burned it with sacred flame, and cracked it with his mace.

docs.title: Icarus
docs.text:
Player: Joe · Ancestry: Human · Class: Warlock

Mercenary

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A soldier-for-hire in his mid-thirties who has plainly had a hard, ugly life and wants you to know it. Broad-shouldered and stout, with a heavy scar carved across his face and one eye left solid white where it passed. Stained black leather, a long black cloak worn thrown back — deliberately, so the scar shows. He does not like people. He says so, repeatedly, without prompting.

He has worked for the Silverbrier family before and has no illusions about them: they hand out the dangerous jobs, they never pay properly, and they never train anyone. He was supposed to have been the security escort for the twelve loggers now missing in the Graylight Forest. He turned up two weeks late. They went in without him.

He has not stopped insisting that this is their fault.

docs.title: The Voice
docs.text:
Icarus's patron has not been named. But when Jonas Dawn offered him the blessing of the Church of the Dawn — "may the light find you" — and Icarus answered that he didn't care about the light, something in his head answered too. A thought that arrived on its own, in a voice that was not his:

Good. We don't want the light.

He has told no one.

docs.title: In Combat
docs.text:
His magic comes out the color of the forest he is standing in — his eldritch blast looks like he has reached down, scooped a handful of the Graylight's fog off the ground, and flung it. Slight gray. It barely shows against the mist.

He carries a bow he keeps reaching for out of habit, over spells that would serve him better. He is fragile — eleven hit points, an armor class of eleven, and a wolf took seven of those hit points out of him in a single bite, on the arm he casts from. He spent the back half of the fight behind a tree.

Known spells: eldritch blast, armor of Agathys, green-flame blade, witch bolt.

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Wilonet — mutual, enthusiastic loathing. She saved him from walking into one of her own hunter's traps, then triggered it with a rock to show him what he'd nearly stepped in. He thanked her. She respected him fractionally more for it. He still hates her exactly as much.
  • Grano — barely spoken. Icarus has decided, on no evidence, that he likes the dwarf so far. The two of them have said almost nothing to each other and both seem content to keep it that way.
  • Jonas Dawn — an old friend, greeted warmly by name. Icarus grabbed him by the shirt and threatened to kill him for information within minutes of sitting down.
  • The Silverbrier head of security — a running, mutual contempt. Icarus cannot negotiate with him, because every conversation ends with "you were two weeks late."

docs.title: Session History
docs.text: Twelve Missing — Appointed himself leader of the party on the grounds that he was supposed to have been guarding these loggers in the first place. Failed to talk the fee up from twenty gold. Assaulted the town healer. Cased Old Marnie's shelves for a theft he has openly announced he intends to commit, and spotted an unidentified bottle of clear, moving liquid there. Was mauled by a blinking wolf, dropped to four hit points, disengaged, and hid behind a tree while the drow he despises finished the fight.

docs.title: Vorhan Aru Nyshara
docs.text:
Player: Gary · Ancestry: Shadar-kai · Class: Druid

Wildfire Druid, Mourning-singer

docs.title: What the Ash Remembers
docs.text:
Long before there were names for any of it, there was only the war — celestial host against fiend host, century folding into century, until the world itself was little more than the ground they fought on. Whole regions burned down to glass. Rivers ran the color of things no one wanted to name. The war did not end because either side won. It ended because the world underneath it was dying, and something older than both armies finally noticed.

The primordials did not take a side. They walked the wreckage afterward the way someone walks through a house after a fire — not to mourn it, but to see what could still be built from what was left. They took the groves that had drunk centuries of spilled blood and breathed elves into the wood. They took the mountains the war had cracked open and woke dwarves in the broken stone. Every race born that way carries a piece of the war's cause in it — a memory of what the fighting was over.

The Shadar-kai are different. No primordial shaped them on purpose. They rose from the ash-fields themselves — the places where nothing grew anymore, where the dead of both armies and neither army had been burned down past distinction. Centuries of grief and heat and unfinished things settled into that ground, and when the primordials came through reweaving the world, something in the ash simply stood up, on its own, before anyone meant for it to. Other peoples call them ash-born, cinder-kin, the war's leftover children. It isn't wrong. The Shadar-kai don't carry a memory of why the war was fought. They carry the memory of how it ended — every ending, all at once, still settling.

That's the inheritance. Not anger. Not even grief, exactly. A people built from the moment after the fire, who know in their bones that ash is not the end of something — it's only the part right before the part where something else gets to grow.

docs.title: The Mourning-singer's Path
docs.text:
Vorhan was raised among those who took that inheritance seriously and turned it into a craft. Mourning-singers don't fight the dying or pretend it isn't happening — they sit with it, and they speak it into something survivable. He learned calligraphy first, the careful work of writing a name down correctly before it could be lost, then herbalism, for the small mercies a body needs at the very end. Later, the words. The old cycle-poems, spoken in a particular cadence, meant to make a room feel less like a wall closing and more like a tide finally allowed to go out.

Because the Shadar-kai belong fully to neither the celestial nor the infernal half of the old war, they were trusted by both to perform last rites for the dying of either side — and so Vorhan grew up fluent in Celestial and Infernal as well as Draconic and Common, a neutral voice at deathbeds that had no business agreeing on anything else. He was good at it. He still is. He has never thought of it as morbid work. Someone has to be the one who isn't afraid of the room.

What he learned slower, and reluctantly, was the fire itself — not the war's fire, the destroying kind, but the kind his own people were made from. Druidic teachers showed him that flame doesn't have to consume to matter. It clears overgrowth so something else can root. It renders fat to ash and the ash feeds the next season's green. He took to it the way he took to the poems: directly, without flinching, already three steps ahead of where the lesson was trying to lead him. His teachers called it impatience. He called it knowing where a thing was going before it got there. Both were probably right.

docs.title: The Thing He Doesn't Talk About
docs.text:
He doesn't tell strangers everything, and there is one thing he doesn't tell anyone. Somewhere in his rites — a name spoken slightly wrong, a verse closed a beat too early, he still isn't certain which — something was left unfinished that should have gone on whole. It hasn't left him since. He doesn't call it a curse. He isn't sure "haunting" is even the right word for something that mostly just waits, just outside the edge of what he can see clearly, the way smoke waits in a room after the fire's already out.

So now there's a ritual he performs every dawn and every dusk, without exception, that cannot be skipped or shortened: he says the names, in order, of everyone he has ever sung into their ending. It isn't superstition to him. It's bookkeeping. It's proof, said out loud twice a day, that the count still holds — that whatever follows him hasn't found a way to add itself to that list yet, or subtract someone he loves from it early.

It is also, not coincidentally, the reason he reads and memorizes poetry that has nothing to do with death at all. He needs somewhere else for his mind to go, sometimes, that isn't the list.

He doesn't know yet whether the thing trailing him is grief that simply never found a shape to settle into, or something the ash itself never finished making. He intends to find out. He intends to end it cleanly, the same way he ends everything — and if he can't end it, he'll see to it that it never reaches the ones he'd build a life with anyway. That's not a vow he's made out loud to anyone yet. It doesn't need saying. It's just true.

docs.title: Fire That Doesn't Consume
docs.text:
People expect a Shadar-kai who works in fire and last rites to be cold, or grim, or quietly furious about all of it. Vorhan finds this mildly funny. He isn't fighting the dark. He's not haunted by rage — he's haunted, full stop, which is a much more specific and much less dramatic problem, and he intends to solve it the way he solves everything: directly, efficiently, and without much patience for theatrics along the way.

His fire is the kind that clears a field, not the kind that burns a city. It's decisive. It's quick to notice what's already over and unwilling to let anyone pretend otherwise out of politeness. It rebuilds as readily as it ends — sometimes in the same breath. He'll talk a stranger out of a bad night with the same calm, unhurried directness he'll use to close out a fight that's already decided. Nothing about him is reluctant. He simply prefers things finished, and he has never once mistaken an ending for a cruelty.

He carries that conviction the way his people carry the ash-fields in their blood: quietly, constantly, and with the settled certainty that whatever burns away clears room for the next green thing to come up through it. He intends to be there to see what grows. He's just not entirely sure yet whether he'll have to burn the thing following him to the ground first, or whether — like everything else he's ever sung to rest — it just needs someone willing to finally say its name correctly.

docs.title: Bond Details
docs.text:
Her name is Kaeth Voss Ilune. She trained beside Vorhan at the same mourning-house, took her vows the same season he did. They were never lovers — something steadier and stranger than that, the kind of bond two people get from years of standing on either side of the same deathbeds, learning to read each other's pauses in the cycle-poems so the verse never broke mid-line. She was better at it than he was, if he's honest. Calmer. She never let her own face show what the room was costing her.

What's happening to her started the same way his did — a name spoken a half-beat wrong, a rite she swears she closed correctly and can't have. She didn't recognize it for what it was until it was already settled in. His version moves fast and stays just out of sight, more presence than symptom. Hers is slower, and worse for being slow: she's started losing names. First the ones from years back, then last season's, and now — in her last letter to him, the handwriting noticeably less steady than the Vorhan remembers — she admitted she'd had to ask someone else what her own teacher was called.

For a mourning-singer, losing the names is the actual death. The list is the work. If it goes, everyone she ever sang to rest goes with it, a second time, properly, and there's no one left to say they mattered.

Vorhan thinks he knows why he's stalled longer than she has — not because he's stronger, but because his daily ritual is exactly the kind of stubborn, repetitive, almost mechanical discipline that seems to be slowing whatever this is down. Two names, every dawn and dusk, no exceptions, for years. She trained differently — looser, more intuitive, trusting her memory instead of drilling it — and it's costing her now. He doesn't know if his method would even transfer. He's never tried teaching it to anyone, because teaching it means explaining why he does it, and that means finally saying the thing out loud he's never said to anyone.

He hasn't gone back yet. Partly because he isn't sure his discipline would help her at all — and turning up empty-handed, after this long, feels like its own kind of cruelty. Partly because some honest, unkind corner of him is afraid of exactly what he'll find if he opens that door and finally has to look at this thing in someone else's face instead of his own.

docs.title: Wilonet
docs.text:
Player: Lindsey · Ancestry: Drow · Class: Rogue

Outlander

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Three hundred years old and comfortably the eldest thing in the party, which she does not let anyone forget. Tall, with pale skin tinted blue-violet, long silver hair, a green hood and cloak, and light metallic armor built for moving quietly. She lives on the outskirts of Timberfall rather than in it, comes into town only when she needs to, and has been ranging the shallow edges of the Graylight Forest long enough to have her own hunter's traps still set out there.

She took the job for money. She wants a new sword. She has been perfectly honest about this from the first minute and has not pretended otherwise since.

docs.title: Temperament
docs.text:
Dry, cutting, and unexpectedly decent underneath it. She refers to Icarus, without affection, as "the smelly one" and "the human," and proposed within five minutes of meeting him that if he slowed them down they should leave him behind and split his share. When she found him about to step into one of her own snares, she admits the thought of letting him do it crossed her mind — and then she stopped him, warned him what her traps look like, and threw a rock to spring it so he'd know for next time.

She speaks Dwarvish. She used it, in front of the Silverbriers' man, to ask Grano privately whether this job was worth taking.

docs.title: In Combat
docs.text:
The most dangerous person in the party by a considerable margin, and the fight in the Graylight made that undeniable. Short sword in hand and a dagger in reserve, a dexterity of 20, and a habit of ending things. She beheaded one blinking wolf with a single clean stroke, and when a second one turned on Grano she took it apart deliberately — opening it from the throat down and spilling it while holding eye contact with the surviving wolf.

It worked. The wolf was afraid of her.

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
• Icarus — she despises him with real energy, and has still saved his life once and bought back the goodwill he destroyed twice. She promised Jonas Dawn she would try to rescue his sister as an apology for Icarus's behavior.
  • Grano — an easy, immediate, wordless respect. They spoke Dwarvish together before they had spoken common. She would rather have his counsel than the human's leadership, and let Icarus "lead" mainly because arguing was beneath her.
  • Old Marnie — a genuine old friend, one of the few she has. She asked the others to let her do the talking and asked them, specifically, not to rough him up.
  • Jonas Dawn — she heard his private grief across a crowded tavern on a natural twenty, and her first instinct was to shout across the room that the price for rescuing his sister had just gone up to forty gold. Her second, later, was to promise to bring the woman home.

docs.title: Session History
docs.text: Twelve Missing — Negotiated, listened, and killed. Recognized the eavesdropping opportunity at the Fat Goat and used it. Got the party its only real information about the forest by taking them to Old Marnie. Kept Icarus out of her own trap. Killed two blinking wolves, one by beheading and one by disembowelment, and frightened a third badly enough that it mattered.
